era_context: The mid-14th century was marked by the Black Death's ongoing devastation across Eurasia and North Africa, fundamentally disrupting social and economic structures. In Europe, the Hundred Years' War between England and France continued; in the Islamic world, the Mamluk Sultanate dominated the Eastern Mediterranean and Levant; in Asia, the Yuan Dynasty in China faced mounting pressure from Han Chinese rebellions that would culminate in the Ming Dynasty's rise; in Africa, the Mali Empire was in decline while the Songhai Empire was consolidating power in West Africa.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; reliance on later oral traditions and archaeological evidence)
Southeast Asia (sparse European documentation; local chronicles less accessible in Western scholarship)
Oceania and Pacific Islands (minimal contemporary written records)

events.title: Battle of Poitiers aftermath and Anglo-French negotiations
events.description: Following the English victory at Poitiers in 1356, the period 1354–1360 saw intensive negotiations and raids. In 1354, English forces under the Black Prince continued campaigns in Aquitaine and Gascony, consolidating territorial gains. The Treaty of Brétigny (1360) would eventually formalize these conquests, but 1354 represents the height of English military advantage during this phase of the Hundred Years' War. The conflict devastated French agriculture and accelerated social fragmentation.
events.certainty_note: Well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary chronicles; military campaigns are attested in multiple sources.

events.title: Ottoman conquest of Gallipoli
events.description: The Ottomans, under Murad I or his predecessors, consolidated control of Gallipoli (Gelibolu) on the European side of the Dardanelles Strait. This strategic fortress gave the Ottomans a permanent foothold in Europe and facilitated further Balkan expansion. The conquest was part of the Ottoman expansion into the Balkans that would accelerate over the following decades, fundamentally altering the geopolitical balance between Christian and Islamic powers in southeastern Europe.
key_figures: Murad I (Ottoman Sultan, r. 1362–1389, though conquest may predate his reign)
events.certainty_note: The exact date of Gallipoli's conquest is debated; sources place it between 1354–1366. 1354 is a traditional date, but some scholars argue for 1366. Contemporary documentation is sparse.

events.title: Zhu Yuanzhang's early rebellion and rise in southern China
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang, who would found the Ming Dynasty, was active in the Red Turban Rebellion against Yuan rule. By 1354, he had established himself as a military commander in the lower Yangtze region, capturing Nanjing in 1356. His rise exemplified the broader collapse of Yuan authority in southern China, driven by famine, plague, and Han Chinese resentment of Mongol rule. His eventual victory would restore Han Chinese rule and establish one of history's most enduring dynasties.
key_figures: Toghon Temür (Toyan Khan, Yuan Emperor, r. 1333–1368)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Yuan Shi (History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Zhu Yuanzhang's military campaigns are well-documented in Ming and Yuan sources, though exact dates of early engagements vary slightly.

events.title: Continued Black Death mortality and social disruption in Europe
events.description: The Black Death, which peaked in 1347–1353, continued to cause significant mortality and social upheaval in 1354. While the acute phase had passed in many regions, recurring waves of plague persisted. Labor shortages drove up wages for surviving workers, accelerating the decline of feudal labor obligations and contributing to peasant unrest. The psychological and social trauma of the plague manifested in religious fervor, flagellant movements, and scapegoating of Jewish communities.

sources.name: Giovanni Boccaccio, Decameron (written 1353–1354, reflects contemporary conditions)

events.certainty_note: Plague's continuation and social effects are extensively documented in contemporary chronicles and later demographic analysis.

events.title: Mamluk Sultanate consolidation under al-Nasir Muhammad
events.description: Al-Nasir Muhammad al-Qalawun (r. 1341–1377, with interruptions) ruled the Mamluk Sultanate during a period of relative stability and cultural flourishing. In 1354, the sultanate was consolidating control over Egypt, the Levant, and the Hejaz, maintaining trade routes and religious authority as custodian of Islam's holiest sites. The Mamluks' military strength and administrative sophistication made them the dominant Islamic power of the era, resisting both Mongol and Ottoman expansion.
events.certainty_note: Al-Nasir Muhammad's reign and policies are well-documented in Mamluk chronicles, though written after his death.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Gallipoli's Ottoman conquest (1354-002) is contested; some scholars argue for 1366 based on Byzantine sources. The significance of Zhu Yuanzhang's early campaigns (1354-003) is sometimes overstated in popular histories; he was one of several rebel commanders, and his eventual dominance was not inevitable in 1354. The Black Death's social effects (1354-004) are often romanticized as uniformly liberating for peasants; in reality, outcomes varied by region, and many survivors faced renewed oppression as lords attempted to restore feudal obligations.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1354 is uneven. European sources (especially French and English chronicles) are relatively abundant, reflecting the Hundred Years' War's prominence in surviving records. Islamic sources are substantial but often compiled centuries later. Chinese sources are rich but reflect Yuan and Ming court perspectives. African, Southeast Asian, and Oceanic history are severely underrepresented in surviving written records; archaeological evidence is sparse. The year 1354 falls within the Black Death's shadow, which dominates European historiography and may obscure other significant developments. Court-sponsored chronicles tend to emphasize political and military events over economic or social change.

graph_edges.note: English military campaigns of 1354 culminated in the Battle of Poitiers (1356)
Gallipoli conquest was part of broader Ottoman Balkan expansion under Murad I
Zhu Yuanzhang's early campaigns led to Ming Dynasty establishment
Labor shortages from plague contributed to Peasants' Revolt in England (1381)
Military campaigns occurred amid ongoing plague disruption
